role_line: Data Scientist · Energy-Access Researcher · Power Systems
paragraphs: I work at the seam where machine learning, geospatial analytics, and power-systems engineering meet — a place where a well-chosen model or a carefully built map can change which communities get electricity and how resilient that electricity is to a changing climate. My work turns large-scale energy datasets into evidence that planners, funders, and utilities can act on.

Most of what I do begins with a spatial question: where are people underserved, what would it cost to reach them, and how do we build systems that keep working after the ribbon is cut? To answer these, I build predictive models, optimization pipelines, and GIS tools — then translate the results into research outputs and dashboards that hold up in a stakeholder meeting, not just a notebook.

My foundation is deliberately hybrid. I hold an M.S. in Electrical & Computer Engineering from Carnegie Mellon University Africa, with a concentration in Applied Machine Learning, Data Analytics, and Intelligent Systems, and a B.Eng. in Electrical & Electronics Engineering from the Federal University of Technology, Minna, concentrating in Control and Energy Systems. The engineering grounds the data science: I know what the numbers mean when the grid is on the other end of them.

That combination is the point. Energy access is not only a modelling problem or only an infrastructure problem — it is both at once, and it lives on a map. I built my training so I could hold all three at the same time.

education.detail: Concentration: Applied Machine Learning, Data Analytics, Intelligent Systems · CGPA 3.79/4.00
